name: Urashima Taro
description: Urashima Taro is a protagonist in a Japanese fairytale. He is a kind fisherman who rescues a turtle, which in turn rewards him by taking him to Ryugu-jo, the Dragon Palace under the sea. There, he is entertained by the princess Otohime and enjoys a life of luxury and pleasure. After what he believes to be only a few days, he expresses a desire to return home to see his aging mother. Otohime reluctantly allows him to leave, gifting him a mysterious box and warning him never to open it. Upon his return, Urashima Taro finds that centuries have passed. His home is gone, his family is dead, and no one remembers him. In despair, he opens the box, releasing a cloud of smoke that ages him rapidly, causing him to die of old age. The tale explores themes of time, mortality, longing, and the consequences of curiosity. Urashima Taro is often seen as a cautionary figure, representing the fleeting nature of life and the importance of cherishing the present.
subtitle: The Fisherman and the Dragon Palace

keyMyths.title: Rescue of the Turtle
keyMyths.description: Urashima Taro saves a turtle from being harmed by children. As a reward, the turtle carries him to the Dragon Palace beneath the sea.

keyMyths.title: Visit to the Dragon Palace
keyMyths.description: Urashima Taro spends what he believes to be a short time in the Dragon Palace, enjoying the hospitality of Princess Otohime. He is treated to feasts, dances, and luxurious accommodations.

keyMyths.title: The Tamatebako Box
keyMyths.description: Upon his departure from the Dragon Palace, Otohime gives Urashima Taro a box called Tamatebako, warning him never to open it. When he opens it in despair upon returning home, he rapidly ages.

sources.author: Donald Keene
sources.description: Provides a selection of classic Japanese tales, including variations of the Urashima Taro story.
sources.text: Provides a selection of classic Japanese tales, including variations of the Urashima Taro story.

sources.author: F. Hadland Davis
sources.description: Offers a retelling of the Urashima Taro legend within a broader collection of Japanese myths.
sources.text: Offers a retelling of the Urashima Taro legend within a broader collection of Japanese myths.

sources.author: Yei Theodora Ozaki
sources.description: Presents a children's version of the Urashima Taro story, suitable for younger audiences.
sources.text: Presents a children's version of the Urashima Taro story, suitable for younger audiences.

associations.name: Turtle
associations.significance: Represents longevity, good fortune, and guidance to the Dragon Palace.

associations.name: Dragon Palace (Ryugu-jo)
associations.significance: Symbolizes a magical, otherworldly realm of pleasure and timelessness.

associations.name: Jeweled Box (Tamatebako)
associations.significance: Represents the passage of time and the inevitable consequences of disrupting the natural order.

associations.name: Princess Otohime
associations.significance: Embodies beauty, generosity, and the allure of the unknown.

associations.name: Time
associations.significance: Illustrates the relativity of time and the dangers of losing touch with one's origins.

associations.name: Nostalgia
associations.significance: Represents the longing for home and the past, even after experiencing great pleasure elsewhere.

cross_cultural_parallels.similarity: Like Urashima Taro, Odysseus experiences a significant time distortion while away from home and faces challenges upon his return.
Oisín visits Tír na nÓg (the Land of Youth) and returns to find centuries have passed, similar to Urashima Taro's experience in Ryugu-jo.
The theme of transformation and a hero's journey to an extraordinary place are also present in Norse myths.

worshipPractices.name: Urashima Shrine
worshipPractices.description: Several shrines across Japan are dedicated to Urashima Taro, where people pray for longevity, safe travels, and good fortune. Offerings often include turtle figurines and depictions of the Dragon Palace.

cultural.modernLegacy: Urashima Taro's tale remains a popular story in Japanese culture, inspiring numerous books, films, anime, and video games. The story serves as a cautionary tale about the importance of cherishing time and remembering one's roots.

extendedContent.title: Early Life
extendedContent.content: Urashima Taro is depicted as a humble fisherman living a simple life by the sea. His days are spent casting nets and providing for his family, showcasing his diligence and connection to the natural world. The tale emphasizes his inherent kindness, establishing him as a virtuous protagonist. One day, while fishing, he encounters a group of children tormenting a turtle. Moved by compassion, Urashima Taro intervenes and releases the turtle back into the ocean. This act of kindness sets the stage for his extraordinary adventure, highlighting the importance of empathy and respect for all living creatures. His early life serves as a foundation for his later experiences, underscoring the theme that even ordinary individuals can encounter extraordinary events.

extendedContent.title: Greatest Quests
extendedContent.content: Urashima Taro's 'quest' is less about overcoming external challenges and more about navigating the complexities of time and identity. His journey to Ryugu-jo, the Dragon Palace, is a reward for his kindness, offering him a life of unimaginable luxury and pleasure. However, this seemingly idyllic existence becomes a test of his character. Despite the allure of eternal youth and beauty, Urashima Taro eventually feels a longing for his home and his aging mother. His decision to return, despite the princess's warnings, demonstrates his unwavering loyalty and sense of responsibility. The opening of the Tamatebako (jeweled box) is not a deliberate act of defiance but rather a consequence of his yearning for the past, marking the end of his extraordinary experience and his return to the mortal realm.

extendedContent.title: Legacy
extendedContent.content: The tale of Urashima Taro has resonated throughout Japanese culture for centuries, becoming a timeless parable about the nature of time, the importance of family, and the consequences of disrupting the natural order. The story serves as a reminder that even the most tempting pleasures cannot replace the bonds of home and family. Urashima Taro's name is synonymous with nostalgia and the bittersweet feeling of returning to a place that has irrevocably changed. The tale continues to be adapted and reinterpreted in various forms of media, ensuring its enduring relevance in contemporary society. Furthermore, Urashima Taro is often invoked in discussions about the concept of time dilation, adding a layer of scientific intrigue to the already captivating myth.

shortDescription: A fisherman saves a turtle and is rewarded with a visit to the Dragon Palace, where he experiences timeless beauty, only to return home and find centuries have passed.
